pick.rationale: Michigan enters as a legitimate national championship contender ranked #2 in both KenPom and Torvik with an elite two-way profile—7th in adjusted offense and 2nd in adjusted defense—while Texas Tech sits 12th-19th in the major metrics, representing an efficiency margin gap of over 13 points per 100 possessions. The Wolverines' suffocating defense (2nd nationally, allowing just 88.4 points per 100 possessions) should neutralize a Texas Tech offense that has been significantly compromised since losing reigning Big 12 Player of the Year JT Toppin to a season-ending ACL tear in February. While Texas Tech's Christian Anderson is available after his groin injury scare and the Red Raiders have had over a month to adjust without Toppin, their 75-53 blowout loss to Iowa State in the Big 12 quarterfinals exposed their ceiling against elite competition. Michigan star Yaxel Lendeborg is dealing with a low ankle sprain but expects to play, and the Wolverines' substantial analytical edge, defensive prowess, and roster depth make this a manageable Sweet 16 matchup with limited upset risk.

reasoningStep.title: Michigan vs Texas Tech Sweet 16 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Evaluated substantial analytical gap between #2 Michigan and #12-19 Texas Tech, factoring in key injuries on both sides including Texas Tech's season-ending loss of JT Toppin and Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg ankle sprain.
evidence: Michigan ranks #2 in both KenPom and Torvik with elite two-way profile (7th offense, 2nd defense) and 31-2 record
Texas Tech ranks 12th in Torvik and 19th in KenPom with 22-10 record, efficiency margin gap of 13+ points per 100 possessions
Texas Tech lost reigning Big 12 Player of the Year JT Toppin to season-ending ACL tear in February, contributing to 75-53 blowout loss to Iowa State in Big 12 quarterfinals
Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg (Big Ten Player of the Year) dealing with low ankle sprain but expects to play
Texas Tech's Christian Anderson and LeJuan Watts both dealing with injuries from Big 12 tournament but Anderson is available

result.text: Michigan Star Yaxel Lendeborg Reveals Injury Status for NCAA Tournament - Detroit Sports Nation About Editorial Disclaimer Ethics Policy Fact-Checking & Corrections Policy Privacy Policy About Editorial Disclaimer Ethics Policy Fact-Checking & Corrections Policy Privacy Policy Search Home Detroit Lions Lions Roster Detroit Tigers Detroit Red Wings Detroit Pistons College Sports Home Detroit Lions Lions Roster Detroit Tigers Detroit Red Wings Detroit Pistons College Sports Home » Michigan Star Yaxel Lendeborg Reveals Injury Status for NCAA Tournament Michigan Star Yaxel Lendeborg Reveals Injury Status for NCAA Tournament By: Don Drysdale Published: Mar 15, 2026, 6:56 pm Modified: Mar 15, 2026, 6:56 pm The Michigan Wolverines appear to have avoided a major injury scare just days before the start of the 2026 NCAA Men’s Basketball Tournament . According to reporter Alejandro Zúñiga , Michigan forward Yaxel Lendeborg revealed that he is dealing with a low ankle sprain , but he expects to play in the Wolverines’ NCAA Tournament opener. That is welcome news for a Michigan team that earned a No. 1 seed in the Midwest Region after posting an impressive 31–3 record during the regular season. A Key Piece of Michigan’s Title Push Lendeborg has been one of the most important players on Michigan’s roster this season. The 6-foot-9 forward has delivered strong production on both ends of the floor and is a major reason why the Wolverines are considered a legitimate national championship contender. During the 2025–26 season , Lendeborg averaged: 14.4 points per game 7.0 rebounds 3.3 assists 50.6% shooting from the field 34.3% from three-point range 82% from the free-throw line His all-around impact helped him earn 2025–26 Big Ten Player of the Year honors , along with All-Big Ten recognition . Why His Health Matters Michigan’s tournament hopes depend heavily on the versatility Lendeborg brings to the lineup. His ability to score inside, stretch the floor, and facilitate offense makes him one of the most complete forwards in the tournament field. If the injury had been more serious, it could have dramatically altered Michigan’s outlook in the bracket. Instead, the early indications suggest the Wolverines will have their star available when March Madness begins. Leave a Reply Cancel reply Your email address will not be published. result.text: Sweet 16 2026: Full Breakdown, Predictions, and Upset Alerts | KnowInsiders --> --> --> March 18, 2026 23:47 | About us | Contact | RSS facebook/knowinsidertoday Home How to Who is Wishes & Quotes Traditions & Beliefs Holidays Explainers Behavior & Psychology Top U.S. Knowledge Hub Home U.S. Knowledge Hub Sweet 16 2026: Full Breakdown, Predictions, and Upset Alerts Larry Mimosa March 18, 2026 | 09:11 Share Tweet Email Share A complete preview of the Sweet 16 round in March Madness 2026, including predictions, likely matchups, and the best upset alerts to watch. The Sweet 16 round in March Madness 2026 The Sweet 16 is still more than a week away, and as of March 18, 2026 , the field has not been set yet . But the bracket is out, the seeds are locked, and the early paths are clear enough to make a smart first read on who looks built for the second weekend — and who could blow up the bracket before then. The official men’s tournament schedule has the Sweet 16 on March 26-27 , with Elite Eight games on March 28-29 . At the top, the bracket has four No. 1 seeds: Duke, Arizona, Florida, and Michigan . Duke is the overall No. 1 seed, while Arizona, Michigan, and defending champion Florida round out the top line. Betting markets have also placed Duke, Michigan, and Arizona near the top of the title board, with Florida close behind, which makes them the safest Sweet 16 projections before the first full round tips off. East Region: Duke has the cleanest headline path and remains the most convincing pick to reach the Sweet 16. The more interesting part of this region sits below it. Kansas is the No. 4 seed, St. John’s the No. 5, Michigan State the No. 3, and UConn the No. 2, creating one of the bracket’s toughest clusters. If you want the safest prediction, Duke and UConn make sense. But St. John’s feels like a live upset threat after finishing in the AP top 10 while still landing only a No. 5 seed. West Region: Arizona looks strong enough to survive the first weekend, and Purdue’s draw makes the Boilermakers another strong Sweet 16 candidate. Gonzaga, however, is the team that could make this region messy. Wisconsin is also worth watching because 5-vs-12 games are historically volatile, and High Point was specifically mentioned by AP as one of the bracket’s Cinderella possibilities. Arizona is still the best bet here, but this region has real upset energy. South Region: Florida and Houston stand out immediately. Florida enters as a No. 1 seed and defending champion, while Houston sits in the second tier of national-title odds and has the profile of a team nobody wants to face early. The first real upset watch here is No. 12 McNeese vs. No. 5 Vanderbilt , one of those classic bracket traps that always gets attention. If chalk holds, a Florida-Houston regional collision would feel entirely believable. Midwest Region: Michigan is the No. 1 seed, but this may be the most delicate path of all four top seeds. Purdue just beat Michigan in the Big Ten title game,

result.text: What Texas Tech basketball's Christian Anderson, LeJuan Watts said of injuries Skip to main content Home High School Red Raiders SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week Texas Tech basketball players offer injury updates after Big 12 Tournament exits Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal KANSAS CITY, Mo. — In the span of about 30 seconds in Thursday's quarterfinal game of the Big 12 Tournament, the Texas Tech basketball team saw Christian Anderson and LeJuan Watts both leave the T-Mobile Center floor with apparent injuries. Watts' came first after he went down in pain holding his foot after an awkward landing. On the next possession, Anderson slipped on the court for the umpteenth time, stayed on the ground and was taken to the end of the bench. By the time of both injuries, Iowa State had opened up the game and coasted to a 75-53 win to advance to the semifinals to send Texas Tech packing. Watts and Anderson both took their regular places on the bench after getting looked at but neither returned to the game. "I'm feeling good," Anderson said in the postgame press conference. "Obviously, the floor is a bit slippery so I think I just kind of mis-stepped or did a movement that caused me to kind of slip and ended up in a little unnatural position. That's what it was." Watts, meanwhile, isn't even entirely sure how he suffered his injury. "I actually don't know," Watts said in the locker room. "We're gonna have to go back. It was either step on someone's ankle or the court, maybe. I'm not sure." Watts also said, "I think I'm just gonna do some rehab for a few days and bounce back." Texas Tech heads to the NCAA Tournament next week with their tournament seed, destination and opponent to be announced Sunday. Michigan Star Yaxel Lendeborg Reveals Injury Status for NCAA Tournament - Detroit Sports Nation About Editorial Disclaimer Ethics Policy Fact-Checking & Corrections Policy Privacy Policy About Editorial Disclaimer Ethics Policy Fact-Checking & Corrections Policy Privacy Policy Search Home Detroit Lions Lions Roster Detroit Tigers Detroit Red Wings Detroit Pistons College Sports Home Detroit Lions Lions Roster Detroit Tigers Detroit Red Wings Detroit Pistons College Sports Home » Michigan Star Yaxel Lendeborg Reveals Injury Status for NCAA Tournament Michigan Star Yaxel Lendeborg Reveals Injury Status for NCAA Tournament By: Don Drysdale Published: Mar 15, 2026, 6:56 pm Modified: Mar 15, 2026, 6:56 pm The Michigan Wolverines appear to have avoided a major injury scare just days before the start of the 2026 NCAA Men’s Basketball Tournament . According to reporter Alejandro Zúñiga , Michigan forward Yaxel Lendeborg revealed that he is dealing with a low ankle sprain , but he expects to play in the Wolverines’ NCAA Tournament opener. That is welcome news for a Michigan team that earned a No. 1 seed in the Midwest Region after posting an impressive 31–3 record during the regular season. A Key Piece of Michigan’s Title Push Lendeborg has been one of the most important players on Michigan’s roster this season. The 6-foot-9 forward has delivered strong production on both ends of the floor and is a major reason why the Wolverines are considered a legitimate national championship contender. During the 2025–26 season , Lendeborg averaged: 14.4 points per game 7.0 rebounds 3.3 assists 50.6% shooting from the field 34.3% from three-point range 82% from the free-throw line His all-around impact helped him earn 2025–26 Big Ten Player of the Year honors , along with All-Big Ten recognition . Why His Health Matters Michigan’s tournament hopes depend heavily on the versatility Lendeborg brings to the lineup. His ability to score inside, stretch the floor, and facilitate offense makes him one of the most complete forwards in the tournament field. If the injury had been more serious, it could have dramatically altered Michigan’s outlook in the bracket. Instead, the early indications suggest the Wolverines will have their star available when March Madness begins. Leave a Reply Cancel reply Your email address will not be published. result.text: Sweet 16 2026: Full Breakdown, Predictions, and Upset Alerts | KnowInsiders --> --> --> March 18, 2026 23:47 | About us | Contact | RSS facebook/knowinsidertoday Home How to Who is Wishes & Quotes Traditions & Beliefs Holidays Explainers Behavior & Psychology Top U.S. Knowledge Hub Home U.S. Knowledge Hub Sweet 16 2026: Full Breakdown, Predictions, and Upset Alerts Larry Mimosa March 18, 2026 | 09:11 Share Tweet Email Share A complete preview of the Sweet 16 round in March Madness 2026, including predictions, likely matchups, and the best upset alerts to watch. The Sweet 16 round in March Madness 2026 The Sweet 16 is still more than a week away, and as of March 18, 2026 , the field has not been set yet . But the bracket is out, the seeds are locked, and the early paths are clear enough to make a smart first read on who looks built for the second weekend — and who could blow up the bracket before then. The official men’s tournament schedule has the Sweet 16 on March 26-27 , with Elite Eight games on March 28-29 . At the top, the bracket has four No. 1 seeds: Duke, Arizona, Florida, and Michigan . Duke is the overall No. 1 seed, while Arizona, Michigan, and defending champion Florida round out the top line. Betting markets have also placed Duke, Michigan, and Arizona near the top of the title board, with Florida close behind, which makes them the safest Sweet 16 projections before the first full round tips off. East Region: Duke has the cleanest headline path and remains the most convincing pick to reach the Sweet 16. The more interesting part of this region sits below it. Kansas is the No. 4 seed, St. John’s the No. 5, Michigan State the No. 3, and UConn the No. 2, creating one of the bracket’s toughest clusters. If you want the safest prediction, Duke and UConn make sense. But St. John’s feels like a live upset threat after finishing in the AP top 10 while still landing only a No. 5 seed. West Region: Arizona looks strong enough to survive the first weekend, and Purdue’s draw makes the Boilermakers another strong Sweet 16 candidate. Gonzaga, however, is the team that could make this region messy. Wisconsin is also worth watching because 5-vs-12 games are historically volatile, and High Point was specifically mentioned by AP as one of the bracket’s Cinderella possibilities. Arizona is still the best bet here, but this region has real upset energy. South Region: Florida and Houston stand out immediately. Florida enters as a No. 1 seed and defending champion, while Houston sits in the second tier of national-title odds and has the profile of a team nobody wants to face early. The first real upset watch here is No. 12 McNeese vs. No. 5 Vanderbilt , one of those classic bracket traps that always gets attention. If chalk holds, a Florida-Houston regional collision would feel entirely believable. Midwest Region: Michigan is the No. 1 seed, but this may be the most delicate path of all four top seeds. Purdue just beat Michigan in the Big Ten title game,

result.text: What Texas Tech basketball's Christian Anderson, LeJuan Watts said of injuries Skip to main content Home High School Red Raiders SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week Texas Tech basketball players offer injury updates after Big 12 Tournament exits Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal KANSAS CITY, Mo. — In the span of about 30 seconds in Thursday's quarterfinal game of the Big 12 Tournament, the Texas Tech basketball team saw Christian Anderson and LeJuan Watts both leave the T-Mobile Center floor with apparent injuries. Watts' came first after he went down in pain holding his foot after an awkward landing. On the next possession, Anderson slipped on the court for the umpteenth time, stayed on the ground and was taken to the end of the bench. By the time of both injuries, Iowa State had opened up the game and coasted to a 75-53 win to advance to the semifinals to send Texas Tech packing. Watts and Anderson both took their regular places on the bench after getting looked at but neither returned to the game. "I'm feeling good," Anderson said in the postgame press conference. "Obviously, the floor is a bit slippery so I think I just kind of mis-stepped or did a movement that caused me to kind of slip and ended up in a little unnatural position. That's what it was." Watts, meanwhile, isn't even entirely sure how he suffered his injury. "I actually don't know," Watts said in the locker room. "We're gonna have to go back. It was either step on someone's ankle or the court, maybe. I'm not sure." Watts also said, "I think I'm just gonna do some rehab for a few days and bounce back." Texas Tech heads to the NCAA Tournament next week with their tournament seed, destination and opponent to be announced Sunday.
